coredata.dc:title: Optoelectronic properties and charge transfer dynamics in graphene quantum dot/Ir(III) nanocomposites: Enhancing photocatalytic performance through strategic BTF conjugate binding and end-capping variations
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Li, Zhiwei
Wu, Qiulan
Hou, Xia
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this study is to analyze the optoelectronic properties and charge transfer kinetics in nanocomposites comprising graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Ir(III) complexes, with a specific focus on their relevance in photocatalytic applications. The investigation explores how the extent of π-conjugation, variations in end-capping substituents, and the binding position of benzothiazole fluorene (BTF) conjugates influence the electronic structures. The results indicate that attaching GQDs alters the band gaps and results in different energy alignments. By adjusting the position of BTF conjugate binding and the end-capping substituent, optimal photocatalytic performance can be achieved. Binding the BTF conjugate at the para-position enhances electron injection into TiO2, thereby improving photocatalysis. Conversely, binding the BTF at the meta-position contributes to the regeneration of the ground state, leading to increased light absorption and photoelectric conversion. Furthermore, incorporating a BTF conjugate at the meta-position of the 2-phenyl pyridine ligand enhances light absorption and photoelectric conversion in the nanocomposites. These findings pave the way for further exploration of the effects of different conjugate binding positions and substituents on the overall properties and potential applications of these nanocomposites.

Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Driving forces of electron injection and ground state regeneration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of intricate characteristics of the nanocomposites formed by the graphene quantum dot (GQD)/Ir(III) complex. The GQD and the Ir(III) complex are intricately connected through a sophisticated amide bond. The formation of the Ir(III) complex involves the attachment of the benzothiazole fluorenyl (BTF) conjugate to either the para- or meta-position of the diimine ligand. Moreover, we have made alterations to the conjugation length (c) of the BTF motif and replaced the benzothiazole (BTZ) substituent with a naphthalimide (NI; d) in order to evaluate the impact of the end-capping substituent on the photocatalytic capability of the nanocomposite.

The depiction of energy levels of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites, showcasing the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The red dashed line represents the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2, while the blue dashed line signifies the HOMO of the I−/I3− redox pair. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. The dashed line represents the Fermi level.
Schematic illustration of charge transfer pathways based on energetic alignment between GQD and Ir(III) complex.

The normalized absorption spectra of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. The absorption spectra are represented by the black, blue, and red lines, which correspond to the nanocomposite, isolated GQD, and isolated Ir(III) complex, respectively. It is important to note that the isolated GQD and Ir(III) complex were extracted directly from the nanocomposite without undergoing any further structural optimization. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Details related to the chemical reactivity parameters, interfragment charge transfer (IFCT), and charge transfer length (ΔL) of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites and the isolated Ir(III) complexes.
Free energy changes associated with electron injection (ΔGinj (eV)) and ground-state regeneration (ΔGreg (eV)) for both the isolated Ir(III) complex and the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.
Energy conversion efficiency (Ƞ (%)) and related photovoltaic parameters, and total hyperpolarizability (κ (a.u.)) for GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Optoelectronic properties and charge transfer dynamics in graphene quantum dot/Ir(III) nanocomposites: Enhancing photocatalytic performance through strategic BTF conjugate binding and end-capping variations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology research laboratory, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this study is to analyze the optoelectronic properties and charge transfer kinetics in nanocomposites comprising graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Ir(III) complexes, with a specific focus on their relevance in photocatalytic applications. The investigation explores how the extent of π-conjugation, variations in end-capping substituents, and the binding position of benzothiazole fluorene (BTF) conjugates influence the electronic structures. The results indicate that attaching GQDs alters the band gaps and results in different energy alignments. By adjusting the position of BTF conjugate binding and the end-capping substituent, optimal photocatalytic performance can be achieved. Binding the BTF conjugate at the para-position enhances electron injection into TiO2, thereby improving photocatalysis. Conversely, binding the BTF at the meta-position contributes to the regeneration of the ground state, leading to increased light absorption and photoelectric conversion. Furthermore, incorporating a BTF conjugate at the meta-position of the 2-phenyl pyridine ligand enhances light absorption and photoelectric conversion in the nanocomposites. These findings pave the way for further exploration of the effects of different conjugate binding positions and substituents on the overall properties and potential applications of these nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique characteristics and advantages of Ir(III) complexes as photocatalysts in various chemical reactions [1,2] have garnered significant attention in recent years. These complexes possess exceptional light absorption and charge transfer excitation properties, enabling efficient charge injection into semiconductors and facilitating photocatalytic processes. Furthermore, they exhibit remarkable thermal stability [3,4], an adjustable electronic structure [5,6], and adaptable redox chemistry [7–9], making them highly suitable for applications in energy conversion and environmental remediation. It is important to note, however, that the excited state properties of Ir(III) complexes are contingent upon the specific ligands coordinated to the Ir(III) ion. For example, Ir(III) complexes that incorporate 2-(2-quinolinyl)quinoxaline ligands and 2-Aryloxazolo[4,5-f][1,10]phenanthroline (N^N) ligands exhibit significant light absorption in the visible range [10,11]. Conversely, ligands with weak π acceptor properties are classified as “weak field” ligands, resulting in a high spin configuration and limited absorption of Ir(III) complexes.

While Ir(III) complexes have demonstrated immense potential in the realm of photocatalysis, surpassing N719 dye in terms of longer excited state lifetimes, exceptional catalytic activity and structural versatility, their energy conversion performance often falls short of that of N719 dye, thereby limiting their widespread application in photocatalysis. The inferior energy conversion efficiency of Ir(III) complexes can be attributed to a number of factors. Firstly, N719 dye boasts a broader absorption range that extends well into the visible region, enabling it to efficiently harness a significant portion of the solar spectrum [12], whereas Ir(III) complexes typically exhibit a narrower absorption range [13,14]. Secondly, N719 dye possesses a higher molar extinction coefficient, signifying its superior ability to absorb light compared to many Ir(III) complexes. Despite the advantages offered by Ir(III) complexes in terms of catalytic activity and structural diversity, their lower energy conversion efficiency relative to N719 dye presents a persistent challenge that necessitates careful design and optimization of the complexes and the photocatalytic system as a whole. The process of enhancing photocatalysts can be achieved through various methods, such as modifying coordinating ligands, adjusting functional groups, and incorporating co-catalysts. An effective strategy involves the utilization of graphene-based materials as co-catalysts alongside Ir(III) complexes [9,15–18]. By integrating these elements, the optical properties of the composite material can be improved, resulting in longer durations of excited states. These improvements hold great potential for significantly boosting the overall efficiency of photocatalysts.

The electronic properties and energy levels of the Ir(III) complex are heavily influenced by the structure of the ligand, which in turn impacts the charge transfer process and overall efficiency of the nanocomposite in photocatalytic applications for energy conversion, environmental remediation, and organic synthesis. Optimizing the ligand structure can lead to improved photocatalytic performance of the Ir(III) complexes. For example, when the benzothiazole fluorene (BTF) motif is attached to the para position of the 2-phenyl pyridine (C^N) ligand, the lifetimes of the triplet excited states of the Ir(III) complex are significantly reduced [19]. This reduction in available charge carriers affects the photocatalytic efficiency. Moreover, increasing the degree of π-conjugation of diimine ligands extends the absorption range into the infrared region, which is desirable for applications in optoelectronics and photovoltaics. The quinoquinoline (quqo) ligand, in particular, exhibits greater π-conjugation compared to bipyridyl, phenanthroline, or 2-pyridyl quinoline ligands. This enhanced π-conjugation further decreases the band gap and causes a redshift in the 1,3metal-to-ligand (MLCT)/1,3ligand-to-ligand (LLCT) band of the Ir(III) complex. In addition, quqo possesses an extra nitrogen atom, which gives it a greater ability to withdraw electrons compared to bisquinoline. As a result, it has the capacity to absorb light more effectively in shorter wavelength regions. A research team led by Sun et al. conducted an experiment where they synthesized four Ir(III) complexes, each with a different placement of the BTF conjugate on the cyclometallating ligand [11]. These complexes displayed wide-ranging absorption capabilities in both the visible and near-infrared regions, depending on the specific location of the BTF conjugate binding on the C^N ligand.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess distinct electronic properties that make them highly attractive for a range of applications. These GQDs can be easily synthesized at a low cost and exhibit exceptional photostability [20–22]. By combining GQDs with dye complexes, hybrid materials can be created that leverage the advantages of both components, thereby overcoming the limitations of dye complexes. To prepare the GQD/dye nanocomposite, an organic dye such as Rhodamine 6G, methylene blue, or methyl orange is dissolved in a solution. The synthesized GQD suspension is then added to the dye solution while stirring, carefully optimizing the GQD:dye ratio to achieve the desired optical and photocatalytic properties. The mixture is allowed to react for a sufficient amount of time to form the nanocomposite, which is subsequently collected through centrifugation [23–25]. Excess reactants are removed by washing the nanocomposite with water or solvents, and it is then dried under ambient conditions or in a vacuum. The interaction between the dye and the oxygen-containing groups on the GQDs results in the formation of an amide linkage [26–29]. This interaction has been further investigated by Li et al., who employed density functional theory (DFT) simulations to study the adsorption behavior of the organic molecule dimethyl (DM) on GQDs. The most favorable outcomes were observed when utilizing amidation linkage, as revealed in their study. In a separate study conducted by Zhao et al., it was found that GQD/ZL006 exhibited the highest effectiveness in enhancing charge transfer efficiency in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [30]. Similarly, Wazzan et al. explored the utilization of nanocomposites consisting of natural dyes and GQDs as sensitizers in DSSCs, resulting in improved charge separation and transfer performance compared to using isolated dye [31]. Wang et al. conducted an examination on the integration of graphene into DSSCs [32]. They discussed the chemical exfoliation technique employed to generate graphene sheets and their ability to undergo chemical functionalization. The authors also provided an explanation of the fabrication process for TiO2/graphene-based DSSCs and analyzed the impact of different graphene concentrations on important performance parameters such as short-circuit current density, open-circuit voltage, and power-conversion efficiency. In conclusion, the combination of dye complex photocatalysts with GQDs holds significant potential for unlocking their full capabilities in various photocatalytic applications, thereby overcoming the limitations associated with Ir(III) complexes.

The versatile electronic and optical properties of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites are crucial for enhancing their effectiveness in various photocatalytic processes. The Ir(III) complex serves as a flexible foundation with distinct electronic and optical properties that can be customized to meet specific needs. By controlling the ligand structure [33,34], charge density [35], and coordination environment [36,37] of the complex, the electronic interactions between the two molecules can be finely adjusted. Furthermore, the π-conjugated structure of GQDs grants greater control over the dynamics of charge carriers in the nanocomposites, based on the precise energy alignment between the two components. This allows for systematic tuning of the nanocomposites' photoluminescence, absorption, and charge-transfer properties to achieve desired results. For instance, Cui and his team discovered that improving the electron-withdrawing capability of the 2-aryl substituent in the nanocomposite facilitates charge transfer during photoexcitation [15]. This discovery holds potential for further exploration in the realm of photocatalytic applications. The remarkable potential of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites lies in their ability to enhance performance through strong coupling. This synergy between the two components has led to advancements in important fields such as solar energy conversion and photodynamic therapy [38–40]. The tunability of electronic and optical properties in these nanocomposites further highlights their significance in both fundamental research and cutting-edge applications.

Through the use of DFT calculations, this study delves into the investigation of the photocatalytic capabilities of nanocomposites containing the GQD/Ir(III) complex. Previous research involved the synthesis of two distinct sets of Ir(III) complexes, each with a BTF conjugate attached at either the para- or meta-position of the aromatic ring of the cyclometalated ligand [11]. By altering the π-conjugation length in the BTF motif and employing different end-capping substituents like naphthalene (NI) and benzothiazolylfluorenyl (BTZ), the effects on the photophysical properties of the Ir(III) complex were examined. This study aims to comprehensively explore the manipulation of optoelectronic properties and charge transfer dynamics in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites to enhance their photocatalytic performance. In contrast to previous works [41,42], this research introduces novel aspects of nanocomposite design and analysis, specifically focusing on the impacts of strategic BTF conjugate binding positions and variations in end-capping substituents that were previously overlooked. The impact of BTF conjugate positioning and end-capping modifications on the electronic structure, charge transfer length, interfragment charge, and photocatalytic efficiency of nanocomposites can be comprehended in a more nuanced manner. While previous research focused on electron-donating/withdrawing groups or generic conjugate ligands, this study offers fresh insights into the significance of BTF binding and end-capping in enhancing photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, it delves deeper into the dynamics of charge transfer at the nanoscale, revealing how subtle changes in structure can optimize charge separation and photocatalytic performance. By introducing novel concepts in nanocomposite design and analysis, this study expands our understanding of tailoring optoelectronic properties and optimizing charge transfer for superior photocatalytic capabilities.

Expanding on our previous investigation, we devised four nanocomposites by connecting GQDs to an Ir(III) complex through amide bonding [41,43], as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The GQD model employed in this study possesses a diameter of approximately 1.5 nm. Experimental synthesis has successfully produced GQDs with diameters ranging from 1 to 4 nm [44–46]. Therefore, our chosen model size of 1.5 nm falls within the feasible range, rendering it a practical and pertinent model for examination. The utilization of a 1.5 nm model is crucial in capturing and analyzing the quantum confinement effects exhibited by GQDs, which display significant size dependence within the 1–4 nm range, as evidenced in prior research [47]. Furthermore, this model size enables comprehensive and high-quality quantum mechanical simulations while keeping computational expenses manageable. Numerous theoretical studies on GQDs and other quantum dot systems also employ models of similar sizes within the 1–4 nm range [25,28–30,48–58]. Although specific quantitative details may differ based on GQD size, our study offers indispensable qualitative insights into the nature of quantum confinement effects. Future investigations on larger systems can benefit from these findings, which are likely applicable to slightly larger GQDs. We name the nanocomposites in the following manner: GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ refers to the GQD/Ir(III) complex with a BTF conjugate positioned at the meta-position of the aromatic ring of the C^N ligand. GQD–Ir–para–BTZ represents the same complex, but with the BTF conjugate positioned at the para-position. GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L indicates a reduced level of π-conjugation in the BTF motif. Finally, GQD–Ir–para–NI replaces the BTZ substituent with an NI substituent when the BTF conjugate is positioned at the para-position. To assess the charge transfer between the nanocomposite and a semiconductor at the interface, a statistical model was developed using a cluster consisting of 38 molecules of TiO2. The bidentate chelating mode is empolyed to attach the nanocomposite to the surface of the (TiO2)38 cluster and has been previously identified as the most stable configuration [43,59]. The lengths of the TiO bonds in the resulting structure align with the findings of prior studies [43,60].

In order to analyze the optoelectronic characteristics, we employed a robust computational approach that incorporated DFT and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT). This method yielded valuable insights into the underlying interactions that govern the electronic and optical properties of the materials. Selecting an appropriate functional and basis set was crucial to ensure accurate results. Hybrid functionals, such as PBE0 [61,62] and PBE0-D3 [63,64], are commonly utilized for simulating GQDs [65–67] and Ir(III) complexes [68–70], as they can effectively capture long-range dispersion effects that play a crucial role in predicting electronic transitions in these nanomaterials. Therefore, we conducted DFT calculations using the PBE0-D3 functional and employed the 6-31G(d,p) basis set for all elements except Ir and Ti, as implemented in the Orca software package [71,72]. This choice strikes a reasonable balance between computational cost and accuracy, enabling us to incorporate the necessary polarization functions for studying the GQD-Ir(III) complex interaction. To accurately capture the electronic and optical properties of materials like Ir(III) complexes, which are known to exhibit significant spin-orbit coupling effects, we have implemented a relativistically recontracted Karlsruhe basis set (DKH-def2-TZVP [73]). This specialized basis set is tailored to account for relativistic effects in heavy element systems such as Ir and Ti. By incorporating the Douglas-Kroll-Hess (DKH) Hamiltonian and a relativistic basis set into our calculations, we are able to provide a more precise representation of these properties.

To ensure the reliability of the chosen functionals, we conducted an assessment comparing the band gaps of pristine GQDs (C36H18) using five different functionals: Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE), Becke's three-parameter hybrid functional with Lee-Yang-Parr correlation (B3LYP), Coulomb-attenuated hybrid DFT functional (CAM-B3LYP), PBE0 hybrid functional, and Wu-Truhlar non-separable hybrid functional with B97X exchange (wb97x). The band gaps calculated using these functionals were found to be 2.82 eV, 3.92 eV, 4.22 eV, 7.01 eV, and 7.67 eV, respectively. For more detailed information, please refer to supplemental Fig. S1. The absence of exact exchange in the PBE functional leads to the smallest calculated band gap. On the other hand, the CAM-B3LYP and wb97x functionals produce significantly larger band gaps compared to PBE0 and B3LYP, primarily because they incorporate higher levels of exact exchange. The significance of exchange contributions in these calculations is underscored by the accuracy of band gap values provided by functionals incorporating moderate levels of exact exchange, such as PBE0 and B3LYP. These functionals yield band gaps ranging from 3.9 to 4.4 eV. These values align closely with other computational results [74–77], reinforcing the accuracy of the hybrid functional. In particular, the PBE0 functional, utilized in this study, has demonstrated its reliability in accurately predicting the excitation properties of graphene-based materials. This is substantiated by the close alignment of the band gap calculated using the PBE0 functional with the tight-binding calculation result of 4.11 eV [78]. The tight-binding approach, previously employed in various studies, has consistently yielded band gap values for GQDs that closely match experimental results [75,79–81]. Consequently, these tight-binding results serve as reliable benchmarks for comparative analysis. Additionally, the PBE0 functional has proven effective in calculating the absorption spectra of Ir(III) complexes. The calculated spectra closely resemble the experimental spectra [68,82–85], further attesting to the functional's accuracy. In summary, the PBE0 functional accurately describes the electronic structure of nanocomposites consisting of GQDs and Ir(III) complexes.

To begin the simulation, we start by optimizing the geometries of each GQD and Ir(III) complex using the designated functional and basis set. This ensures that they are in their lowest energy conformation. Once this is done, we proceed to create the nanocomposite and optimize its geometry. After that, we perform single-point energy calculations to determine the electronic structure and ground state characteristics of the nanocomposite. To examine the excited-state properties and simulate the optical absorption spectrum, we employ TDDFT calculations with the chosen functional and basis set. Through these calculations, we are able to identify the electronic transitions that influence the optical properties of the nanocomposite, providing valuable insights into its potential for optoelectronic applications.

The efficiency of a nanocomposite's photocatalytic performance relies heavily on two critical factors: the length of charge transfer (ΔL) and the interfragment charge (ΔQ). These factors are essential in determining the overall effectiveness of the photocatalytic process. They play crucial roles in separating and transporting the generated charge carriers (electrons and holes) within the nanocomposite material. As a result, various redox reactions are initiated, leading to the successful degradation of pollutants or the conversion of solar energy into other valuable forms. In this study, the Multiwfn software package was utilized to obtain the values of ΔL and ΔQ [86].

The effectiveness of chemical reactions is dictated by the surface characteristics of the constituent substances. A surface that is highly reactive improves the ability to attract and activate molecules involved in the reaction, resulting in superior performance in photocatalysis. Specifically, the chemical hardness (α) quantifies how resistant a substance is to changes in its electron density. In the context of photocatalytic systems, higher chemical hardness generally indicates a reduced propensity for electron transfer or charge separation. This can have a detrimental effect on photocatalytic performance since the generation of electron-hole pairs and the separation of charges are crucial steps in the process. On the other hand, the chemical potential (β) represents the potential energy of electrons in a material and is linked to its ability to undergo reduction or oxidation reactions. In the realm of photocatalysis, materials with lower β are more likely to engage in redox reactions, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance. Lastly, the electrophilicity index (γ) serves as a measure of a material's inclination to accept electrons, reflecting its interaction with electron donors (nucleophiles) and electron acceptors (electrophiles). Materials that exhibit higher γ possess a greater propensity to accept electrons, thereby facilitating the generation of electron-hole pairs and augmenting the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions. Conversely, materials characterized by low γ have the potential to impede photocatalytic performance due to hindered electron transfer and limited redox reactions at the active sites. Ultimately, by meticulously considering these factors, one can strategically design and customize nanocomposite materials with desired properties to attain optimal photocatalytic performance. For more detailed information on the calculation methodology for α, β, and γ, please refer to the relevant reference papers [31, 87].

The nanocomposites in Fig. 2 exhibit a remarkable effect on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels when GQDs are attached. This attachment unveils the impressive potential for the regeneration of the ground state in photoexcited Ir(III) complexes. The unique combination of lower HOMO levels and higher LUMO levels in Ir(III) complexes, compared to the I−/I3− redox pair (−4.8 eV [43]) and the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 (−4.0 eV [43]), contributes to this potential. Several factors, including the positioning of the BTF conjugate, the length of π-conjugation in the BTF motif, and the type of end-capping substituents, have diverse effects on the HOMO and LUMO levels. For example, shifting the binding position of the BTF conjugate from meta- to para- destabilizes the HOMO level but stabilizes the LUMO level of the Ir(III) complex. Similarly, changing the end-cap substituent from BTZ to NI stabilizes the HOMO level but destabilizes the LUMO level. Furthermore, reducing the π-conjugation length destabilizes both the HOMO and LUMO levels. The levels in Ir–meta–BTZ experience destabilization due to the presence of GQDs, but this can be alleviated by reducing the conjugation length of the BTF motif in GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L. By decreasing the degree of π-conjugation of ligand, the electronic interaction between GQDs and the Ir(III) complex is diminished, resulting in the stabilization of the HOMO and LUMO levels. Therefore, the HOMO and LUMO levels of the Ir(III) complex are influenced in various ways by the degree of π-conjugation, end-capping substituents, and the binding position of BTF. The incorporation of GQDs into Ir(III) complexes modifies these effects, highlighting the significant impact of GQD attachment on the HOMO and LUMO levels of the nanocomposites compared to the Ir(III) complexes themselves.

The analysis of a molecule's charge distribution and electronic structure is made easier through the use of projected density of states (PDOS) and frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs). PDOS provides information on the contributions of the HOMO and LUMO, while FMOs illustrate the distribution of electrons and holes, which impact reactivity. For nanocomposites where the BTF conjugates are positioned at the para- and meta-positions of Ir(III) complexes, alongside a reduced π-conjugation length of the BTF motif (see Fig. 3(a)-(c)), the Ir(III) complexes primarily govern the HOMOs and LUMOs. Further details can be found in supplemental Table S1. The resulting energy alignment influences the band gaps of these nanocomposites. Interestingly, the band gap of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ is larger than that of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, which in turn is larger than that of GQD–Ir–para–NI, despite the fact that the band gap of Ir–meta–BTZ is smaller than that of Ir–meta–BTZ–L. This discrepancy is attributed to the charge redistribution within nanocomposite, modifying the band gap trend in the nanocomposites with respect to their Ir(III) complex counterparts. The positioning of the BTF conjugate has a direct impact on the energy alignment, showcasing the ability to fine-tune the electronic properties of these nanocomposites. In the GQD–Ir–para–NI, the LUMO is solely controlled by the Ir(III) complex, while the HOMO is influenced by both GQD and the Ir(III) complex. By replacing the end-capping substituent with a NI group, the HOMO of the Ir(III) complex becomes stabilized. As a result, the FMOs of the nanocomposite are predominantly influenced by GQD and the Ir(III) complex.

When examining the energy configurations of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ, GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, and GQD–Ir–para–BTZ, it becomes evident that charge transfers take place from the GQD to the Ir(III) complexes upon photoexcitation (Fig. 4 ). This discovery establishes the GQD as a co-sensitizer that enhances the photocatalytic efficiency of the Ir(III) complexes. Specifically, the GQD transfers electrons to the Ir(III) complex's LUMO, while holes are transferred to the HOMO, resulting in charge separation within the nanocomposite. However, in the case of GQD–Ir–NI, photoexcited holes are shared between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex, whereas electrons transfer from the GQD to the Ir(III) complex. This process achieves GQD charge separation and amplifies photoexcited electrons in the Ir(III) complex, ultimately optimizing the photocatalytic performance. The behavior of photoexcited holes is contingent upon the degree of delocalization. The energy level arrangement in GQD-Ir-NI also suggests a deceleration in the recombination of electron-holes (LUMO→HOMO of the nanocomposite) compared to the other three molecular structures. This deceleration arises from a reverse electron transfer from the Ir(III) complex to the oxidized GQD, a process impeded by the interfacial and intermolecular distance. This slowed rate proves advantageous for the injection of electrons from the Ir(III) complex into the semiconductor.

The absorption spectra of four nanocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 5 . By examining the absorption spectra of isolated GQDs and Ir(III) complexes extracted from the nanocomposites, without any additional geometry optimization, we can gain insight into the individual components' contributions to the absorption spectra. In the nanocomposite, two prominent peaks (Smax1 and Smax2; also referred to in supplemental Table S2) are observed. These peaks primarily involve ligand-localized 1π,π* transitions, with minor contributions from 1MLCT, 1LLCT, or intraligand charge transfer (1ILCT). For Smax2 in GQD-Ir-meta-BTZ, there is a slight indication of intermolecular charge transfer between the Ir(III) complex and GQD. The lowest excited state (S1) exhibits characteristics of 1,3LLCT. When the BTF conjugate binds from the meta- to para-position, the optical spectra experience a blueshift, and intermolecular charge transfer transitions in GQDs transform into π,π* transitions. The varying excited state properties of GQD–Ir–para–BTZ and GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ can be attributed to the different degrees of electron and hole delocalization upon initial excitation. Additionally, reducing the conjugation length of the coordinating ligand (in GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L) has minimal impact on the positions of the Smax1 and Smax2 states but alters their relative intensities. The normalized intensity of the Smax2 state in the Smax1 state shows an increase when comparing GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ, indicating an improved ability to harvest high-energy light in the presence of a shorter ligand conjugation length. By replacing the BTZ substituent with the NI substituent, there is a redshift in Smax1 and a significant decrease in its relative intensity compared to Smax2. This suggests that enhancing the electron-withdrawing capacity of the end-capping substituent reduces the light-harvesting ability in the low-energy region but enhances it in the high-energy region for the nanocomposite.

Interfacial charge transfer (IFCT) is a vital process in nanocomposite photocatalysts, where electrons move between constituents upon light absorption. Effective IFCT separates photogenerated electrons and holes, preventing recombination and promoting migration to active sites. It facilitates electron movement towards the cathode and hole movement towards the anode, enhancing redox reactions. Heterojunctions generate inner electric fields that aid charge separation. Selecting nanocomposites with complementary optoelectronic properties maximizes IFCT, significantly enhancing photocatalytic activity. The behavior of different nanocomposites is illustrated in Table 1 , showcasing varying degrees of charge transfer from GQD to Ir(III) complex upon photoexcitation, with GQD–Ir–para–NI exhibiting the highest charge transfer, followed by GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, and GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ. Upon initial excitation, a reverse charge transfer is observed from the Ir(III) complex to GQD in the case of GQD–Ir–para–BTZ, suggesting that the directional charge transfer between Ir(III) complex and GQD can be altered by varying the binding position of the BTZ conjugate, due to the redistribution of charges. However, when the BTZ substituent is replaced with a less potent electron-withdrawing group like the NI group in GQD–Ir–para–NI, the direction of charge transfer changes once again, aligning with the patterns seen in GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L and GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ. This change can be attributed to the influence of the combined states of the complex and GQD on the HOMO of GQD–Ir–para–NI. Consequently, hole delocalization occurs at the HOMO level upon initial excitation, resulting in a greater dispersion of holes within the complex. This suggests that electron transfer takes place at the HOMO level, from GQD to the Ir(III) complex.

The charge transfer length (ΔL) is the distance a charge carrier (electron or hole) can travel within a material before recombining. In photocatalysis, charge carriers are generated when electrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band upon light absorption, leaving behind holes. Efficient photocatalytic performance requires separating these charge carriers and transporting them to reactive sites on the nanocomposite surface to prevent recombination. A longer ΔL enhances photocatalytic performance by promoting charge carrier generation, separation, and migration to reactive sites, resulting in more redox reactions and effective pollutant degradation or enhanced solar energy conversion. Table 1 illustrates the ΔL values of the nanocomposites: GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L (4.46 Å) < GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ (7.20 Å) < GQD–Ir–para–BTZ (8.19 Å) < GQD–Ir–para–NI (9.12 Å). The GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L nanocomposite exhibits the smallest ΔL due to reduced electron delocalization. Furthermore, replacing the BTZ substituent with the NI substituent increases charge separation, indicating that reducing the electron-withdrawing ability of the end-capping substituent enhances charge separation in the nanocomposite.

Overall, the GQD–Ir–para–NI exhibits the highest interfragment charge transfer, along with the greatest charge transfer length upon initial excitation. These characteristics contribute to its enhanced charge separation within nanocomposite, resulting in improved photocatalytic performance.

The computed chemical reactivity parameters of both the nanocomposite and the isolated Ir(III) complex are presented in Table 1. In general, the nanocomposites exhibit lower chemical hardness compared to the isolated Ir(III) complexes, indicating that the attachment of GQD increases their susceptibility to electron transfer or charge separation. However, when the BTF conjugate changes its binding position from para- to meta-, the chemical hardness of the nanocomposite becomes greater than that of the isolated Ir(III) complex, highlighting the significant impact of the binding configuration on the softness of the nanocomposite. The chemical potential of the nanocomposites is similar to that of the isolated Ir(III) complexes, except for GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ, suggesting that the presence of GQD does not significantly influence the redox power of the nanocomposites. Furthermore, except for GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ, the electrophilicity index of the nanocomposites is higher than that of the isolated Ir(III) complexes, indicating a higher likelihood of accepting electrons. This characteristic is advantageous for photocatalytic reactions. By altering the binding location of the BTF conjugate in GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ, there is a consequent redistribution of charge that diminishes the nanocomposite's capacity to receive electrons. This finding aligns with its elevated chemical hardness.

Overall, the GQD–Ir–para–BTZ exhibits relatively lower chemical hardness and chemical potential but a higher electrophilicity index. These characteristics make it favorable for electron injection into TiO2 and thus enhance the photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Driving forces of electron injection and ground state regeneration
ce:section.ce:para: In photocatalysis, electron transfer from the photoexcited photocatalyst to a semiconductor initiates chemical reactions. The efficiency of electron injection depends on the driving force, which can be improved by aligning energy levels between the photocatalyst and semiconductor, promoting desired chemical transformations and enhancing photocatalytic activity. Ground state regeneration, another critical process, restores the photoexcited photocatalyst to its original state after electron injection, ensuring sustained activity. Driving forces for regeneration can be achieved through charge recombination or electron transfer. Optimizing factors such as energy level alignment, environmental conditions, co-catalysts, and photocatalyst properties is essential for maximizing photocatalytic performance. Previous studies have provided an explanation of how the calculation of free energy change can determine the driving forces behind electron injection and ground-state regeneration [43]. Specifically, the Ginj parameter quantifies the driving force for electron injection, whereas the Greg parameter quantifies the driving force for ground-state regeneration.

The data presented in Table 2 demonstrate that the formation of nanocomposites has a notable impact on the electron injection and ground-state regeneration processes. Specifically, it reduces the driving force for electron injection and increases the driving force for ground-state regeneration. Interestingly, the positioning of the BTF conjugate has a minimal effect on electron injection when considering the isolated Ir(III) complex. However, when the BTF conjugate is bound to the para-position, it facilitates faster electron injection to TiO2 compared to binding it to the meta-position. In terms of ground-state regeneration, the formation of nanocomposites has a minimal influence on GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L and GQD–Ir–para–BTZ, relative to their Ir(III) complex counterparts. However, it does lead to a decrease in the driving force for GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ and an increase in the driving force for GQD–Ir–para–NI. This discrepancy can be attributed to the binding of the BTF conjugate at the para-position, which does not significantly affect the HOMO of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite (refer to Fig. 2). On the other hand, binding the BTF conjugate at the meta-position of the aromatic ring in the C^N ligand destabilizes the HOMO of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite, as previously explained.

Overall, the GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ demonstrates the highest driving force for electron injection, while GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L demonstrates the highest driving force for ground-state regeneration. This suggests that attaching the BTF conjugate at the meta-position of the C^N ligand enhances electron injection, while reducing the π-conjugation of the coordinating ligand facilitates ground-state regeneration.

The photocatalytic performance of Ir(III) complexes relies heavily on the efficiency of energy conversion. The key to efficient energy conversion lies in the absorption of light, the creation of excited states, and the effective utilization of these states for productive processes, including electron injection and energy transfer.

Improved overall efficiency can be achieved through a higher energy conversion efficiency, which allows for a greater utilization of absorbed light to fuel photocatalytic reactions. This, in turn, leads to a higher yield of desired chemical reactions. On the other hand, low efficiency results in energy loss and decreased activity. To enhance efficiency, various strategies can be employed, such as making structural modifications, tuning energy levels, and incorporating co-catalysts or sensitizers. By maximizing the energy conversion efficiency, Ir(III) complexes are able to effectively utilize absorbed light energy and drive desired chemical transformations with greater efficiency.

The energy conversion efficiency (η) is ascertained by the open circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current (Jsc), and fill factors (FF). The calculation of this efficiency can be accomplished by employing the subsequent equation [88]:
(1)
η =
V oc

P in
where the input power or irradiance is denoted by Pin; the Voc can be estimated by the difference in energy between the LUMO of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite and the CBM of TiO2 [87,89]. The Voc is closely connected to the electron lifetime. The electron lifetime influences the efficiency of charge collection: a longer electron lifetime provides more time for the electron to be collected at the electrode before recombination. This reduces recombination losses and enhances the overall efficiency of the solar cell. Consequently, a longer electron lifetime generally results in a higher open-circuit voltage. In this investigation, we optimize the geometry of the first singlet excited state of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite to achieve the lowest excitation energy and oscillator strength. We then utilize the following formula to assess the electron lifetime [90,91]:

(4)

where q is the elementary charge (1.6 × 10−19C); LHE(λ) is the photon flux density (number of photons per unit area per unit time per unit wavelength); φinj represents the injection efficiency; tinj refers to the time it takes for a charge carrier (e.g., an electron) to be injected from an external source into the material or system. It depends on factors such as the potential barrier height and the presence of any additional barriers that need to be overcome for injection; trelax represents the time it takes for the injected charge carrier to relax or equilibrate within the material or system and set to be 10 ps according to experimental measurements [93]. During relaxation, the charge carrier undergoes processes such as energy redistribution, thermalization, and interaction with other charges or phonons in the material; γcoll denotes the collection efficiency; ttrans refers to the time it takes for a charge carrier (e.g., an electron) to travel through the material or system from the point of injection to the point of collection and set to be 5 ps according to experimental measurements [94]. It depends on factors such as the mobility of the charge carriers and the distance they need to travel; trec represents the time it takes for charge carriers to recombine or lose their electrical potential energy within the material or system. Recombination can occur through processes such as electron-hole recombination or charge carrier trapping; Is(λ) represents the spectral response of the solar cell. Therefore, to obtain the Ƞ one need to determine the LHE, tinj and trec.

represents the fraction of photons not absorbed by the material at the specific wavelength. By subtracting this fraction from 1, one obtains the LHE, which represents the fraction of incident photons at that wavelength that are absorbed by the material.

where the value 658 is a constant factor. The larger the value of Γ (i.e., broader PDOS), the smaller the value of tinj, indicating a faster injection process.

Eq. (9) calculates ℏΓ, involving a summation over i. Within the summation, pi represents the orbital percentage in TiO2, and ϵi represents the energy of the ith state. The second part of the equation calculates the weighted average energy by summing pi multiplied by ϵi. The absolute difference between ϵ
i and the weighted average energy is multiplied by pi for each term and summed over all terms to obtain ℏΓ. This equation quantifies the broadening of energy levels by measuring the weighted average difference between each state and the overall average energy.

We use Marcus equation to obtain the trec:

where the Boltzmann constant (kB) plays a crucial role as a fundamental parameter. The ambient temperature and the reorganization energy are represented by the symbols T and λ, respectively. The free energy difference between the initial and final states is denoted by ΔG. The electronic coupling (V) characterizes the interaction between the initial and final states involved in the recombination process. Eq. (11) incorporates the constants A and β, which are associated with the strength and distance of the interaction. This equation captures the exponential decay of potential energy as a function of the distance (r) between the donor and acceptor molecules. For more detailed calculations of ΔG, λ, A, and β, please refer to the provided references [30, 43, 95, 96].

Table 3 illustrates the tinj values as follows: GQD–Ir–para–BTZ (0.38 fs) < GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L (0.65 fs) < GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ (0.82 fs) < GQD–Ir–para–NI (3.96 fs). Changing the end substituent from BTZ to the NI group increases the charge injection time due to the reduction in the driving force of electron injection. Additionally, when the BTF conjugate is attached to the meta-position (GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ), it leads to a longer charge injection time compared to when it is attached to the para-position (GQD–Ir–para–BTZ). This finding contradicts the analysis from the previous section, which suggests that the driving force of electron injection in GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ is greater than that in GQD–Ir–para–BTZ. The discrepancy between electron injection time and the driving force of electron injection can be explained using the Marcus theory [97–99]. The nonradiative electron transfer is influenced by changes in free energy, reorganization energy, and electron coupling. In the case of ultrafast electron injection from the dye complex to the TiO2 surface, the reorganization can be disregarded [100,101]. Therefore, electron transfer in nanocomposite@TiO2 hybrid system is primarily driven by changes in free energy and electronic coupling [43]. Electronic coupling's influence on electron injection was examined by analyzing the LUMO orbital of the Ir(III) complex (Supplemental Table S3). A considerable electron density distribution was found in the Ir(III) complex of GQD–Ir–para–BTZ, resulting in enhanced electronic coupling and quicker nonradiative electron transfer than GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ. Furthermore, the trec values are much longer than ttrans, while the tinj values are orders of magnitudes faster than trelax, implying that the charge collection and injection efficiencies can be considered as unity. Hence, Eq. (4) can be simplified as:
(12)
J sc
= ∫ q ∙ LHE

dλ

Such simplification has been previously used for simulating short circuit current density of carboxylated GQDs [92] and dye complex [102–104] and the obtained simulation results are in good agreement with experimental data.

The impact of electron recombination time, electron injection time, and excited electron lifetime on the short-circuit current density in a GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite can be understood as follows: A longer electron recombination time provides more time for electrons to move to the electrode, thereby augmenting the photocurrent. Similarly, faster electron injection, which is the transfer of electrons from nanocomposite to TiO2, increases the photocurrent because more electrons can reach the electrode before they recombine with holes. Moreover, a longer excited electron lifetime, which is the duration an electron remains in an excited state before reverting to the ground state, also enhances the short-circuit current, since the prolonged excited state allows more electrons to reach the electrode. The short-circuit current density (Jsc) of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ and GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L is greater than that of GQD–Ir–para–BTZ and GQD–Ir–para–NI due to longer electron recombination and excited electron lifetimes, as well as a shorter electron injection time. Although GQD–Ir–para–BTZ has a shorter electron injection time than GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ and GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, it also exhibits a shorter electron recombination time and excited electron lifetime. These factors result in a smaller electron density being injected into TiO2, leading to a smaller Jsc than the other two molecular systems.

The open-circuit voltage (Voc) in the nanocomposite is influenced by several factors. A longer electron recombination time can potentially increase the Voc as fewer electrons will have recombined with holes at a given time, leading to higher charge separation. Additionally, rapid electron injection from the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite to the TiO2 due to shorter injection times might result in a higher concentration of electrons in the acceptor, hence potentially a higher Voc. Similarly, a longer excited electron lifetime, which accommodates more electrons in an excited state at any given moment, can potentially enhance the Voc. The overall impact on Voc heavily depends on the interplay among these factors and the specific properties of the nanocomposite materials. The Voc of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ is greater than that of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, GQD–Ir–para–BTZ, and GQD–Ir–para–NI due to a longer electron recombination time and excited electron lifetime. Even though GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L has longer trec and tex than GQD–Ir–para–BTZ and GQD–Ir–para–NI, its Voc is the same as that of GQD–Ir–para–NI and only slightly greater than GQD–Ir–para–BTZ. This indicates that more nonradiative recombination events occurred within GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, which reduces the electron density in CBM of TiO2. On the other hand, Jsc primarily depends on the number of absorbed photons and the efficiency of charge carrier collection. Furthermore, GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L exhibits a reduced degree of π-conjugation. The degree of π-conjugation has two opposing impacts on the nonradiative recombination of the dye complex. On one hand, the decrease in π-conjugation leads to a reduction in electron delocalization, thereby diminishing nonadiabatic coupling and decelerating nonradiative decay as predicted by the time-dependent Schrödinger equation. On the other hand, the decrease in π-conjugation decreases the rigidity of the ligand, resulting in an increase in nuclear wavefunction overlapping and enhancing nonradiative decay according to Fermi's golden rule [42,105]. The overall rate of nonradiative decay depends on the interplay between these two effects. Therefore, in the case of GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L, the latter effect plays a significant role in facilitating faster nonradiative recombination compared to other molecular systems.

The calculated energy conversion efficiencies (Ƞ) are as follows: GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ > GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L > GQD–Ir–para–NI > GQD–Ir–para–BTZ. This indicates that Jsc plays a major role in determining Ƞ. According to Eq. (12), Jsc is mainly regulated by light collection efficiency (LHE). For GQD–Ir–para–BTZ, the optical spectrum is blue-shifted compared to GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ and GQD–Ir–meta–BTZ–L (see Fig. 5 (a)-(c)), which reduces the LHE due to the decreased absorption range. In the case of GQD–Ir–meta–NI, the oscillator strengths of the main absorption peaks are smaller than the others, resulting in a smaller LHE. Moreover, the calculated Ƞ falls within the range of 1–10 % [106–108], which are the typical energy conversion efficiencies of a dye complex. This suggests that Ir(III) complex greatly influences the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite's photoelectric conversion, as it predominantly governs the nanocomposite's HOMO and LUMO (supplemental Table S1). Specifically, the BTF conjugate's attachment at the C^N ligand's meta-position increases light absorption efficiency, thereby improving the nanocomposite's photoelectric conversion.

The energy conversion efficiency of the nanocomposite is directly influenced by its hyperpolarizability [109]. A higher hyperpolarizability indicates a greater capacity to display nonlinear optical effects such as second harmonic generation (SHG) and two-photon absorption (TPA). These nonlinear optical processes allow for the efficient conversion of light energy into other forms of energy by leveraging the intense interactions between light and matter that occur within the nanocomposite. When photons are absorbed by the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite, the strong interactions between light and matter lead to excitations and charge transfer processes within the material [110]. The hyperpolarizability enhances these processes by amplifying the intensity of the induced electric dipole moments and producing higher-order nonlinear optical effects [39]. Consequently, the nanocomposite exhibits enhanced properties of light absorption and emission, facilitating efficient energy conversion [111]. Furthermore, the hyperpolarizability influences the response time of the material to an external electric field [112]. A higher hyperpolarizability results in a faster response, enabling the nanocomposite to quickly adapt to changes in the intensity or polarization of the incident light. This responsiveness is crucial for applications where precise control over energy conversion is required. By increasing the hyperpolarizability of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite, we can significantly enhance the energy conversion efficiency [109]. The enhanced nonlinear optical effects, in conjunction with efficient light absorption and emission properties, lead to a more effective utilization of incident light energy [113]. This has direct implications for various applications of energy conversion, including solar cells, photocatalysis, and optoelectronic devices.

The discrepancies observed in the total hyperpolarizabilities of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites and isolated Ir(III) complexes indicate that the inclusion of GQD results in reduced electron delocalization and decreased polarization of electron density. This observation presents a deviation from previous findings [42], where the addition of GQD enhanced electron delocalization. The main distinction between these two lies in the alignment of energy levels. In the former case, the GQD predominantly housed the HOMO, while the LUMO was located on the Ir(III) complex, promoting charge transfer and increased polarization. In contrast, the present study demonstrates that both the HOMO and LUMO are primarily governed by the Ir(III) complex, while the GQD's conduction and valence band minima act as confinement potentials. This arrangement is likely to restrict charge transfer between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex, leading to reduced electron delocalization. These findings suggest that subtle variations in energy level alignments during the formation of composites can have a significant impact on electronic properties such as hyperpolarizability.

This study offers valuable insights into optimizing the photocatalytic performance of nanocomposites comprising GQDs and Ir(III) complexes. Our findings demonstrate that the inclusion of GQDs in these complexes has a significant impact on the levels of both HOMO and LUMO. The distinct arrangement of these levels suggests the potential for efficient regeneration of ground states following photoexcitation. Furthermore, we have explored how the position of the BTF conjugate, the π-conjugation of the coordinating ligand, and the end-capping substituents can influence the HOMO and LUMO levels. Interestingly, the introduction of GQDs also modifies the trends in the band gap compared to the corresponding Ir(III) complexes. Photoexcitation facilitates the transfer of electrons and holes from GQDs to the Ir(III) complexes, resulting in charge separation within the nanocomposite system.

Additionally, an analysis of the optical properties reveals that the absorption spectra of the nanocomposites display two distinct peaks, with the position and intensity influenced by the binding position of the BTF conjugate and the electron-withdrawing capability of the end-capping substituent. Furthermore, reducing the conjugation length of the BTF motif (GQD-Ir-meta-BTZ-L) improves light absorption, as indicated by the higher normalized intensity ratio between the two main excitation peaks. Meanwhile, utilizing the NI end-capping substituent instead of BTZ enhances charge separation, with the GQD-Ir-para-NI nanocomposite demonstrating the longest charge transfer length of 9.12 Å. Attaching the benzothiazole fluorene (BTF) conjugate at the para-position (in GQD-Ir-para-BTZ) promotes a faster electron injection time (0.38 fs) into TiO2 compared to the meta-position (0.82 fs for GQD-Ir-meta-BTZ). This can be attributed to enhanced electronic coupling arising from the greater electron density distribution (57 %) in the LUMO orbital of the Ir(III) complex. In contrast, binding the BTF conjugate at the meta-position facilitates more effective ground-state regeneration, with GQD-Ir-meta-BTZ exhibiting a driving force of −0.99 eV compared to −0.98 eV for GQD-Ir-para-BTZ. These impacts quantitatively correlate with the calculated energy conversion efficiencies, with GQD-Ir-meta-BTZ exhibiting the maximum efficiency of 6.65 % owing to its optimal light harvesting capability. The computational approach applied provides critical insights to guide synthetic optimizations for enhancing photocatalytic performance. Specifically, the strategic BTF binding configurations and end-capping variations unlock tunability in the optoelectronic properties and charge transfer dynamics to harness the full potential of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.
